In [37]:
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.stats.stattools import durbin_watson
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import het_breuschpagan
import statsmodels.api as sm
from scipy.stats import jarque_bera
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression,LassoCV
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score, make_scorer
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, TimeSeriesSplit, cross_val_score, RandomizedSearchCV
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit, cross_val_score
STEP 0 Load the Data set
In [38]:
""" Import the data, handle missing values, and check column types. """

df = pd.read_excel("Excel_files\\All_Unemployment_Indicators.xlsx")

# Remove rows with NaN values
df = df.dropna()

# Define target variable / dependent
y = df["Unemployment_rate_pct"]

# Define predictors (exclude Quarter but keep it in df) , Independent 
X = df.drop(columns=["Unemployment_rate_pct", "Quarter"])
# Define predictors

# Check data
print(df.head())
print(df.describe())
print(df.isnull().sum())

print("Dataset shape:", df.shape)
print(df.head())
print(X.columns)

STEP 1 Baseline Linear Regression (OLS)

Fit a simple Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Is a simple method used in statistics and data analysis to find the best straight line that explains the relationship
between variables. OLS finds the straight line that fits these points the best.
Purpose: get a first idea of how the predictors relate to the target.

In [39]:
# Add constant for intercept
X_const = sm.add_constant(X)

# Fit OLS model
model = sm.OLS(y, X_const).fit()

print(model.summary())

In [40]:
"""
Significant Variables (Your regression suggests unemployment is mainly driven by:

| Variable          | p-value | Interpretation |
| ----------------- | ------- | -------------- |
| Activity_rate_pct | 0.000   | strong         |
| Inflation_yoy_pct | 0.000   | strong         |
| Interest          | 0.000   | strong         |
| Oil Price         | 0.007   | significant    |

Not significant variables: 

| Variable               | p-value |
| ---------------------- | ------- |
| GDP_growth_pct         | 0.675   |
| VolumeIndex_growth_pct | 0.435   |
| Avg_weekly_hours       | 0.419   |
| Index_Of_Wage          | 0.505   |
| Economic Sentiment     | 0.173   |
Important: this does not mean they are useless — nonlinear models (Random Forest) may still use them.
"""
Out[40]:
'\nSignificant Variables (Your regression suggests unemployment is mainly driven by:\n\n| Variable          | p-value | Interpretation |\n| ----------------- | ------- | -------------- |\n| Activity_rate_pct | 0.000   | strong         |\n| Inflation_yoy_pct | 0.000   | strong         |\n| Interest          | 0.000   | strong         |\n| Oil Price         | 0.007   | significant    |\n\nNot significant variables: \n\n| Variable               | p-value |\n| ---------------------- | ------- |\n| GDP_growth_pct         | 0.675   |\n| VolumeIndex_growth_pct | 0.435   |\n| Avg_weekly_hours       | 0.419   |\n| Index_Of_Wage          | 0.505   |\n| Economic Sentiment     | 0.173   |\nImportant: this does not mean they are useless — nonlinear models (Random Forest) may still use them.\n'
STEP 2 Check Model Assumptions / Errors

a) Durbin-Watson Test (Independent Errors)¶

The Durbin-Watson test checks if the residuals (errors) in a regression are independent over time.

In [41]:
dw = durbin_watson(model.resid)
print("Durbin-Watson statistic:", dw)

Durbin-Watson statistic: 0.9044218543799177
In [42]:
"""
How to interpret the result
Durbin-Watson	Interpretation 
~2	No autocorrelation (good) 
< 1.5	Positive autocorrelation
> 2.5	Negative autocorrelation

0.90 → strong positive autocorrelation

This means the errors are correlated across time,
In practice it means:
The unemployment rate this quarter is strongly related to the previous quarter.
This violates the OLS independence assumption.
But this is not a problem for machine learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost). It mainly affects classical regression inference.
"""
Out[42]:
'\nHow to interpret the result\nDurbin-Watson\tInterpretation \n~2\tNo autocorrelation (good) \n< 1.5\tPositive autocorrelation\n> 2.5\tNegative autocorrelation\n\n0.90 → strong positive autocorrelation\n\nThis means the errors are correlated across time,\nIn practice it means:\nThe unemployment rate this quarter is strongly related to the previous quarter.\nThis violates the OLS independence assumption.\nBut this is not a problem for machine learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost). It mainly affects classical regression inference.\n'
In [43]:
# A lag variable is a past value of the same variable.
# If we create lag 1, we shift the variable one period back
#Economic variables change slowly

df["Unemployment_lag1"] = df["Unemployment_rate_pct"].shift(1)  #This moves the column down by one row.
df["Unemployment_lag2"] = df["Unemployment_rate_pct"].shift(2)
df["Unemployment_lag3"] = df["Unemployment_rate_pct"].shift(3)

# Remove first row created by lag
df = df.dropna()
print(df[["Unemployment_rate_pct", "Unemployment_lag1", "Unemployment_lag2", "Unemployment_lag3"]].head(5))

   Unemployment_rate_pct  Unemployment_lag1  Unemployment_lag2  \
4              15.266667          15.033333          14.766667   
5              15.566667          15.266667          15.033333   
6              13.733333          15.566667          15.266667   
7              13.700000          13.733333          15.566667   
8              13.733333          13.700000          13.733333   

   Unemployment_lag3  
4          16.000000  
5          14.766667  
6          15.033333  
7          15.266667  
8          15.566667
In [44]:
"""
By making Unemployment_lag1, we let the model remember last quarter’s unemployment. This helps it understand that unemployment usually 
changes slowly over time. Without this, the model would only look at current economic data and miss how unemployment naturally moves 
from one quarter to the next.
"""
Out[44]:
'\nBy making Unemployment_lag1, we let the model remember last quarter’s unemployment. This helps it understand that unemployment usually \nchanges slowly over time. Without this, the model would only look at current economic data and miss how unemployment naturally moves \nfrom one quarter to the next.\n'

b) Residual Plot¶

This checks for homoscedasticity (constant variance of errors)

In [45]:
fitted = model.fittedvalues
residuals = model.resid

plt.scatter(fitted, residuals)
plt.axhline(y=0)
plt.xlabel("Fitted values")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.title("Residuals vs Fitted")
plt.show()
In [46]:
""" The Residuals vs Fitted plot checks if prediction errors are random — a random scatter around zero means the model fits well.
 In this case they are more or less good sign"""
Out[46]:
' The Residuals vs Fitted plot checks if prediction errors are random — a random scatter around zero means the model fits well.\n In this case they are more or less good sign'

d) QQ-Plot¶

A QQ-Plot checks if the residuals (errors) of a model follow a normal distribution. If the points roughly follow the straight line, the residuals are close to normal.

In [47]:
sm.qqplot(model.resid, line='s')
plt.title("QQ Plot of Residuals")
plt.show()
In [48]:
""""Good: points follow the straight line"""
Out[48]:
'"Good: points follow the straight line'

e) Jarque-Bera¶

The Jarque–Bera test checks if the residuals (errors) follow a normal distribution.

In [49]:
jb_stat, jb_pvalue = jarque_bera(model.resid)

print("Jarque-Bera statistic:", jb_stat)
print("p-value:", jb_pvalue)

Jarque-Bera statistic: 0.566306927608554
p-value: 0.7534041587191451
In [50]:
""" | p-value | Interpretation                     |
    | ------- | ---------------------------------- |
    | > 0.05  | Residuals are normally distributed |
    | < 0.05  | Residuals are not normal           | 
    
Jarque-Bera statistic: 0.58 → very small, meaning little deviation from normality.
p-value: 0.753 (> 0.05) → we fail to reject the null hypothesis.  
Conclusion:
The residuals appear normally distributed, so the normality assumption of the regression model is satisfied.
    """
Out[50]:
' | p-value | Interpretation                     |\n    | ------- | ---------------------------------- |\n    | > 0.05  | Residuals are normally distributed |\n    | < 0.05  | Residuals are not normal           | \n\nJarque-Bera statistic: 0.58 → very small, meaning little deviation from normality.\np-value: 0.753 (> 0.05) → we fail to reject the null hypothesis.  \nConclusion:\nThe residuals appear normally distributed, so the normality assumption of the regression model is satisfied.\n    '

Summary:

Assumption Result Comment
Independent errors ❌ Some positive autocorrelation (DW = 0.90) Was corrected by creating lags
Homoscedasticity ✅ Approximately normal Appear normally distributed
Normal errors ✅ Approximately normal Residuals roughly follow a straight line in QQ-Plot
STEP 3 Check Multicollinearity

a) Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) Analysis¶

In [51]:
# Add constant
X_const = sm.add_constant(X)   # adds a column named 'const' with all 1s

# Create an empty DataFrame to store the VIF results
vif = pd.DataFrame()
# Add the variable names (column names) to the DataFrame
vif["Variable"] = X_const.columns
# Compute VIF for each variable
# variance_inflation_factor checks how much a variable is explained by the other variables
# High VIF (>10) means strong multicollinearity
vif["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X_const.values, i) for i in range(X_const.shape[1])]

print(vif)

                    Variable           VIF
0                      const  47529.686023
1             GDP_growth_pct      2.165507
2     VolumeIndex_growth_pct      1.771300
3  Avg_weekly_hours_per_week     42.597297
4          Activity_rate_pct      6.200141
5          Inflation_yoy_pct      1.452898
6              Index_Of_Wage     51.296361
7         Economic Sentiment      1.827569
8                   Interest      3.614463
9                  Oil Price      2.357222
In [52]:
"""
The model may have collinearity issues with Avg_weekly_hours_per_week and Index_Of_Wage.
"""
Out[52]:
'\nThe model may have collinearity issues with Avg_weekly_hours_per_week and Index_Of_Wage.\n'

b) Correlation map¶

In [53]:
def plot_correlation_heatmap(df):
    """
    Plots a correlation heatmap of all numeric variables in the dataframe.
    Automatically ignores non-numeric columns like 'Quarter'.
    """
    # Select only numeric columns
    numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include="number")
    
    # Compute correlation matrix
    corr_matrix = numeric_df.corr()
    
    # Plot heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))
    sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, 
                annot=True,
                cmap="coolwarm",
                fmt=".2f",
                linewidths=0.5)
    plt.title("Correlation Matrix of Macroeconomic Variables")
    plt.show()
In [54]:
plot_correlation_heatmap(df)
In [55]:
""" 
-1   strong negative relationship
0    no relationship
+1   strong positive relationship

| Correlation | Interpretation           |
| ----------- | ------------------------ |
| > 0.9       | Severe multicollinearity |
| 0.7-0.9     | High correlation         |
| < 0.7       | Usually acceptable       |

Look especially for correlations involving:
Index_Of_Wage
Avg_weekly_hours_per_week
Activity_rate_pct 

"""
Out[55]:
' \n-1   strong negative relationship\n0    no relationship\n+1   strong positive relationship\n\n| Correlation | Interpretation           |\n| ----------- | ------------------------ |\n| > 0.9       | Severe multicollinearity |\n| 0.7-0.9     | High correlation         |\n| < 0.7       | Usually acceptable       |\n\nLook especially for correlations involving:\nIndex_Of_Wage\nAvg_weekly_hours_per_week\nActivity_rate_pct \n\n'
In [56]:
# Step 0: Handle multicollinearity by creating a composite variable
# Combine the three highly correlated features into a single Labor_Index
#df["Labor_Index"] = df[["Index_Of_Wage", "Avg_weekly_hours_per_week", "Activity_rate_pct"]].mean(axis=1) *** 

# Drop the original highly correlated columns
#df = df.drop(columns=["Index_Of_Wage", "Avg_weekly_hours_per_week", "Activity_rate_pct"])

df = df.drop(columns=["Index_Of_Wage", "Avg_weekly_hours_per_week"])

# Optional: Remove any rows with missing values (if there are NaNs after creating lags)
df = df.dropna()
print(df.head())

STEP 4 Train/Test Split
In [58]:
print(df.columns)
# Step 1: Define the target variable (what we want to predict)
# In this case, the unemployment rate
y = df["Unemployment_rate_pct"]

# Step 2: Define the predictor variables (features)
# Drop the target column because it should not be used as an input
#X = df.drop(columns=["Unemployment_rate_pct"])
X = df.drop(columns=["Unemployment_rate_pct", "Quarter"])

# Step 3: Create a chronological split of the dataset
# 80% of the data will be used for training and 20% for testing
split_index = int(len(df) * 0.8)

# Step 4: Training data (first 80% of the observations)
# We use iloc to keep the time order of the data
X_train = X.iloc[:split_index]
X_test = X.iloc[split_index:]

# Step 5: Testing data (last 20% of the observations)
# This simulates predicting future data
y_train = y.iloc[:split_index]
y_test = y.iloc[split_index:]

# Step 6: Print the number of observations in each dataset
# This helps verify the split was done correctly
print("Training observations:", len(X_train))
print("Testing observations:", len(X_test))

Index(['Quarter', 'GDP_growth_pct', 'VolumeIndex_growth_pct',
       'Activity_rate_pct', 'Inflation_yoy_pct', 'Unemployment_rate_pct',
       'Economic Sentiment', 'Interest', 'Oil Price', 'Unemployment_lag1',
       'Unemployment_lag2', 'Unemployment_lag3'],
      dtype='str')
Training observations: 96
Testing observations: 24
STEP 5 Build Multiple Models

📈 Linear Regression Model¶

In [59]:
# Create the Linear Regression model
# This initializes the regression algorithm
lr = LinearRegression()

# Train (fit) the model using the training data
# The model learns the relationship between the predictors (X_train)
# and the target variable (y_train)
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Make predictions using the test data
# The trained model estimates unemployment for the unseen observations
y_pred_lr = lr.predict(X_test)

# Calculate evaluation metrics to measure model performance

# RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
# Measures the average prediction error, giving more weight to large errors
rmse_lr = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_lr))
# MAE (Mean Absolute Error)
# Measures the average absolute difference between predicted and real values
mae_lr = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_lr)

# R² (R-squared)
# Shows how much of the variation in unemployment is explained by the model
r2_lr = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_lr)

🧮 Ridge Regression Model¶

In [60]:
# Create the Ridge Regression model
# alpha is the regularization parameter that controls the penalty strength
# A larger alpha increases regularization and shrinks coefficients more
ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0)

# Train (fit) the model using the training dataset
# The model learns the relationship between the predictors (X_train)
# and the target variable (y_train) while applying regularization
# to reduce multicollinearity and prevent overfitting
ridge.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict
y_pred_ridge = ridge.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate
rmse_ridge = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_ridge))
mae_ridge = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_ridge)
r2_ridge = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_ridge)

✂️ Lasso Regression Model¶

In [61]:
# This finds the best alpha automatically for lasso 

lasso = LassoCV(cv=5)
lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)

print(lasso.alpha_)

0.037084579305583766
In [62]:
# Create the lasso Regression model

#0.037
#lasso = Lasso(alpha=0.01)
lasso = Lasso(alpha=0.037)
lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred_lasso = lasso.predict(X_test)

rmse_lasso = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_lasso))
mae_lasso = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_lasso)
r2_lasso = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_lasso)

🌲 Random Forest Regressor¶

In [63]:
# Create the Random Forest regression model
# Random Forest is an ensemble model that combines many decision trees

rf = RandomForestRegressor(
    # Number of trees in the forest
    # More trees usually improve stability but increase computation time
    
    n_estimators=1000,
    # Maximum depth of each tree
    # Limiting the depth helps prevent overfitting
   
    min_samples_leaf=3,
    max_depth=10,
    # Fixes the randomness so the results are reproducible
    random_state=42
)

# Train (fit) the model using the training data
# The model learns patterns between the predictors (X_train)
# and the target variable (y_train)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict
y_pred_rf = rf.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate
rmse_rf = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_rf))
mae_rf = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_rf)
r2_rf = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_rf)

⚡ XGBoost Regressor¶

In [64]:
# Create the XGBoost regression model
# XGBoost is a gradient boosting algorithm that builds trees sequentially
xgb = XGBRegressor(
    # Number of boosting rounds (number of trees)
    # More trees can improve performance but increase training time
    n_estimators=150,
    # Maximum depth of each tree
    # Limits complexity to prevent overfitting
    max_depth=2,
    # Step size shrinkage used in update to prevent overfitting
    # Smaller values slow down learning but can improve performance
    learning_rate=0.1,
    # Ensures reproducibility by fixing the random seed
    random_state=42,
    reg_lambda=1, 
    reg_alpha=0.1,
    subsample=0.8,
    colsample_bytree=0.8
)

# Train (fit) the model using the training data
# The model learns the relationship between predictors (X_train)
# and the target variable (y_train) using gradient boosting
xgb.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict
y_pred_xgb = xgb.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate
rmse_xgb = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_xgb))
mae_xgb = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_xgb)
r2_xgb = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_xgb)
STEP 6 Model Evaluation and Comparision
In [65]:
# Collect all results in a dictionary
results = {
    "Model": ["Linear Regression", "Ridge Regression", "Lasso Regression",
              "Random Forest", "XGBoost"],
    "RMSE": [rmse_lr, rmse_ridge, rmse_lasso, rmse_rf, rmse_xgb],
    "MAE": [mae_lr, mae_ridge, mae_lasso, mae_rf, mae_xgb],
    "R2": [r2_lr, r2_ridge, r2_lasso, r2_rf, r2_xgb]
}

# Convert dictionary to DataFrame
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results)

# Display the table
print(results_df)

               Model      RMSE       MAE        R2
0  Linear Regression  0.588067  0.481879  0.729811
1   Ridge Regression  0.585504  0.478625  0.732161

In [66]:
# Optional: nicer style
sns.set(style="whitegrid")

results = {
    "Model": ["Linear Regression", "Ridge Regression", "Lasso Regression",
              "Random Forest", "XGBoost"],
    "RMSE": [rmse_lr, rmse_ridge, rmse_lasso, rmse_rf, rmse_xgb],
    "MAE": [mae_lr, mae_ridge, mae_lasso, mae_rf, mae_xgb],
    "R2": [r2_lr, r2_ridge, r2_lasso, r2_rf, r2_xgb]
}

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
In [67]:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

custom_palette = ["#52357B", "#5459AC", "#648DB3"]

# Melt dataframe for Seaborn
results_melt = results_df.melt(
    id_vars="Model",
    value_vars=["RMSE", "MAE", "R2"],
    var_name="Metric",
    value_name="Value"
)

# Identify best R2
best_model = results_df.loc[results_df["R2"].idxmax(), "Model"]

sns.set_theme(style="dark")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14,7))

# Dark background
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#1E1E2F")
ax.set_facecolor("#1E1E2F")

# Plot bars
bars = sns.barplot(
    x="Model",
    y="Value",
    hue="Metric",
    data=results_melt,
    palette=custom_palette
)

# Title and labels
plt.title("Model Performance Comparison", fontsize=22, weight="bold", color="#E6E6FA")
plt.xlabel("Machine Learning Model", color="#E6E6FA", fontsize=14)
plt.ylabel("Metric Value", color="#E6E6FA", fontsize=14)
ax.tick_params(colors="#E6E6FA")
plt.xticks(rotation=30)

# Value labels
for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    color = "white" if p.get_height() < results_df["R2"].max() else "#FFD700"  # gold for best R2
    ax.annotate(f'{height:.2f}',
                (p.get_x() + p.get_width()/2., height),
                ha='center', va='bottom',
                fontsize=10,
                color=color,
                weight="bold" if color=="#FFD700" else "normal")

# Highlight best R2 bar with overlay rectangle
for i, model in enumerate(results_df["Model"]):
    if model == best_model:
        ax.axvspan(i-0.4, i+0.4, color="#FFD700", alpha=0.15)

# Legend styling
legend = plt.legend(title="Metric")
plt.setp(legend.get_texts(), color="white")
plt.setp(legend.get_title(), color="white")

sns.despine()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [68]:
""""
Linear, Ridge, and Lasso regression:
These three linear models perform very well, with R² values above 0.72.
Lasso Regression gives the best performance overall: lowest RMSE (0.557), lowest MAE (0.476), and highest R² (0.756).
Ridge is very close to Linear Regression, showing that regularization slightly improves performance.
Random Forest and XGBoost:
These tree-based models perform worse in this case.
R² values are around 0.50 or lower, and RMSE and MAE are higher than the linear models.
This suggests that for this dataset, linear relationships dominate, and tree-based models may be overfitting or not capturing additional signal.
Conclusion:
Lasso Regression appears to be the best model for this dataset.
Regularization in Lasso helps reduce overfitting and improves predictive accuracy slightly compared to standard Linear Regression.
Non-linear models like Random Forest or XGBoost do not improve performance, likely because the relationships in your macroeconomic dataset are largely linear.
"""
Out[68]:
'"\nLinear, Ridge, and Lasso regression:\nThese three linear models perform very well, with R² values above 0.72.\nLasso Regression gives the best performance overall: lowest RMSE (0.557), lowest MAE (0.476), and highest R² (0.756).\nRidge is very close to Linear Regression, showing that regularization slightly improves performance.\nRandom Forest and XGBoost:\nThese tree-based models perform worse in this case.\nR² values are around 0.50 or lower, and RMSE and MAE are higher than the linear models.\nThis suggests that for this dataset, linear relationships dominate, and tree-based models may be overfitting or not capturing additional signal.\nConclusion:\nLasso Regression appears to be the best model for this dataset.\nRegularization in Lasso helps reduce overfitting and improves predictive accuracy slightly compared to standard Linear Regression.\nNon-linear models like Random Forest or XGBoost do not improve performance, likely because the relationships in your macroeconomic dataset are largely linear.\n'
In [69]:
# Compute errors
errors = y_test.values - y_pred_lasso
std_error = np.std(errors)

# Upper and lower bounds for shading
upper = y_pred_lasso + std_error
lower = y_pred_lasso - std_error

# X-axis: quarters
quarters = df["Quarter"].iloc[len(df)-len(y_test):]

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

# Plot actual and predicted
plt.plot(quarters, y_test.values, label="Actual Unemployment", marker='o')
plt.plot(quarters, y_pred_lasso, label="Predicted Unemployment (Lasso)", marker='x')

# Plot shaded error region
plt.fill_between(quarters, lower, upper, color='gray', alpha=0.3, label="±1 Std. Dev.")

plt.xlabel("Quarter")
plt.ylabel("Unemployment Rate (%)")
plt.title("Actual vs Predicted Unemployment — Lasso Regression")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
STEP 7 Check for Overfitting

a) Compare Train vs Test Performance¶

In [70]:
# Predictions on training data
y_train_pred = lasso.predict(X_train)

# Metrics on training set
rmse_train = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_train, y_train_pred))
mae_train = mean_absolute_error(y_train, y_train_pred)
r2_train = r2_score(y_train, y_train_pred)

# Metrics on test set
rmse_test = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_lasso))
mae_test = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_lasso)
r2_test = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_lasso)

print("Train RMSE:", rmse_train, "Test RMSE:", rmse_test)
print("Train MAE:", mae_train, "Test MAE:", mae_test)
print("Train R2:", r2_train, "Test R2:", r2_test)

Train RMSE: 0.8677132071740835 Test RMSE: 0.5516265828371286
Train MAE: 0.7209098950591449 Test MAE: 0.43070107061232105
Train R2: 0.8205332416660766 Test R2: 0.7622590650939736

Analysis of Results

The model shows good predictive performance, with R² = 0.81 on the training set and R² = 0.76 on the test set, indicating that the model explains a large proportion of the variability in unemployment. The RMSE and MAE values are lower on the test set than on the training set, suggesting that the model generalizes well to unseen data. Overall, the relatively small difference between training and testing metrics indicates that there is no strong evidence of overfitting, and the model appears to have good predictive stability.

Overfitting (which does not seem the case)

Metric Training Set Test Set
RMSE / MAE very low high
R² very high (close to 1) low

b) Cross-Validation¶

In [71]:
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# R² cross-validation
cv_r2 = cross_val_score(lasso, X, y, cv=5, scoring='r2')
print("Cross-validated R²:", cv_r2)
print("Mean R²:", np.mean(cv_r2))

Cross-validated R²: [0.55594248 0.45525624 0.53740721 0.62494037 0.76225907]
Mean R²: 0.58716107269458

Analysis of Results
The cross-validation results show R² values between 0.39 and 0.76 across the folds, with a mean R² of 0.57. This indicates that the model generally explains a moderate portion of the variance in unemployment, although performance varies depending on the data split. Overall, the results suggest that the model has reasonable predictive ability but some variability across samples, which is common in macroeconomic time-series datasets with limited observations.

c) Check Residuals¶

In [72]:
residuals = y_test - y_pred_lasso
plt.scatter(y_pred_lasso, residuals)
plt.axhline(0, color='red', linestyle='--')
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.title("Residuals vs Predicted — Lasso Regression")
plt.show()

Interpretation
Random distribution of points
The residuals are scattered randomly around 0, which is a good sign.
This suggests the model is not missing a clear pattern in the data.

Residual range
Residuals between -1 and 1 indicate that prediction errors are relatively small.
This means the model predictions are fairly close to the actual values.

No visible pattern
Since the points do not form a curve, funnel, or systematic shape, it suggests no strong heteroscedasticity or model bias.
Short conclusion you can use in your report
The residual plot shows that errors are randomly scattered around zero, with values mostly between -1 and 1. This indicates that the Lasso regression model fits the data reasonably
well and does not exhibit obvious systematic errors or patterns in the residuals.
